data.title: SPTI: Oil The Key Factor In The Inflation Fight
data.description:
The SPDR® Portfolio Intermediate Term Treasury ETF (NYSEARCA:SPTI) has a decent duration of around five years with a modest expense ratio of 0.03%. This is a lot more efficient in terms of expense ratios compared to the iShares comparable duration ETFs also exposed to Treasuries, which have a 0.15% expense ratio. In terms of the appeal of duration at the moment, the key matter of the oil prices as they related to the tensions of the Middle East has been settled for now, and we think this will have a critical effect on keeping inflation sufficiently in check to prevent major duration risks to duration bets like the SPTI.

SPTI Breakdown

The duration of the SPTI is just below 5 years, and appears to be a good option in terms of costs compared to the iShares ETFs of similar duration. This is a Treasury ETF, so we effectively ignore credit risk considerations, even though the U.S. Government is not AAA rated anymore. There is still a spread between AA rated corporate debt and Treasuries, which remain the benchmark, and might as well continue to be considered risk-free, to the extent that they've always been (not been).

CPI is still moderate. There isn't much MoM movement despite tariff effects ranging from the base 10% to the higher tariffs that exist on specific products, and perhaps the reciprocal tariffs that may come into effect at the end of the month absent trade deals with major trading nations like Japan.

A big part of the limited growth in CPI has been that oil prices (CO1:COM) (CL1:COM) have been significantly reduced compared to last year's comps. The significance of a $10 decline in oil prices equates to around a 0.5% negative vector on CPI. Oil is down around $15 per barrel as of today. The Iran-Israel exchange of fire put pressure on oil supply as speculators considered a risk to the passage of oil through the Strait of Hormuz, which concerns more countries than just Iran, and accounts for a very significant portion of global oil supply.

If oil prices remained at those elevated levels, CPI could have been bumped up almost by a whole percentage point. 2.4% is almost within the 2% target level, but 3.4% would have been a considerable level of inflation that the Fed would have to treat with the utmost gravity. This would preclude any possibility of rate cuts whatsoever, as the wage-price and other flywheel effects would have remained at large and threatened persistently high inflation.

Bottom Line

Even at 2.4% the economy is still not out of the woods. But moderate levels, that should look better on a more sustained basis, where American expectations about inflation will not be affected by higher gas prices at the pump, with jobs data still relatively under control in terms of continued openings for jobs and limited unemployment growth, mean that Fed cuts are possible. Openings for jobs unexpectedly rose and firings are still pretty limited, which means corporates are not yet in trouble.

The market clearly agrees with this view, given the recent translations downward of the yield curve. Continued yield curve movements downward are clearly what one would hope for when taking positions in higher duration fixed income instruments. As such, there would be risks to SPTI in that issues in the Middle East could flare up once again and threaten oil movements. But for it to become a problem for the American consumer to the point where it changes the calculation for the Fed would require a more sustained problem, though the Fed may already be more conservative with using its tools to accommodate the economy, given that there remain heightened risks of tensions as the Israel-Hamas war is still ongoing. The Israel-Iran situation, though, seems to be at a geopolitical equilibrium, also based on the position of foreign powers, allies and backers, and given that the Iranian regime was put in serious trouble and was forced to relent.

But for the expectations factor of inflation, the spot price of oil and the price of oil at the pump are what matters. So long as CPI can continue at these more moderate levels, the consciousness of the possibility of a sub 2% inflation rate from the consumer remains a realistic assumption and gives space to rate cutting hopes.

data.title: Strategic Education: Debt-Free, Scalable, And Ready For Growth
data.description:
After a five-day road trip with my wife in the countryside, I'm back and ready to analyze yet another promising EdTech for you.

And you know what's funny? The last time we checked out an EdTech, it turned out pretty well. QuantaSing Group Limited (QSG) has already jumped over 100%, all thanks to its smart focus on the ‘Silver Economy’. Sure, the acquisition of Letsvan helped a ton—it marked their official move into consumer goods and was a big reason for the rally.

But here's the thing: there are still a bunch of overlooked names out there trading at dirt-cheap levels. You just need to look beyond the usual Duolingo, Inc. (DUOL) and Pearson plc (PSO) and you might see some undercovered gems.

Today we’re diving into Strategic Education, Inc. (NASDAQ:STRA)—a bit of a sleeper in the market.

Despite having a market cap north of $2 billion, it’s flying under the radar. No Seeking Alpha analyst has touched it in nearly two years. Let's roll up our sleeves and do it then.

Starting where it all began, Strategic Education's foundation dates back to the founding of Strayer Business College in Baltimore, Maryland back in 1892. The focus was to meet the growing demand for accounting professionals following the productivity boom at the turn of the century.

In 2018, another company crossed paths with Strayer. I’m talking about Capella Education Company.

Unlike Strayer, Capella was all-in on online learning, offering everything from undergraduate to master’s and even doctoral programs. That merger is what gave rise to the Strategic Education we know today.

The deal was worth $1.9 billion and was completed with stock only. Strategic Education was formed with Strayer shareholders owning 52% and Capella shareholders owning 48%, keeping the universities as separate institutions.

This merger caused revenues to grow more than 57% year over year in FY 2019, reaching $1 billion the following year.

But was it just the merger with Capella that sparked that big revenue jump? Definitely not. If I left out their international expansion, I’d be leaving out a huge part of the story.

In mid-FY 2020, Strategic Education completed the acquisition of Laureate Education’s (operator of Torrens University, Think Education, and Media Design School) operations in Australia and New Zealand, expanding well beyond the United States.

To get these assets in its pocket, the EdTech company shelled out just over $600 million, partly financed by issuing new shares and long-term debt that has already been repaid to date.

Alright, alright. We’ve already covered the brands, but what about the products? That’s the cherry on the cake. Strategic’s offerings make it clear that the company is laser-focused on corporate education.

That’s what really sets it apart: building B2B partnerships to help companies upskill their workforce.

We can’t help but mention Sophia Learning, an online platform that offers college-credit courses designed to be affordable and self-paced with a focus on career progression.

But perhaps the real icing on the cake is WorkForce Edge.

In short, it is an educational benefits management platform aimed at companies that offer financial support to employees in courses and training. However, strategically speaking, it is much more than that.

  • It's basically a steady, reliable way to bring in B2B revenue (corporate students generally have higher retention and lower default rates). Companies want to boost employee skills through tuition assistance, and Strategic makes that easy by centralizing everything on one platform. It also helps employers track progress and manage reimbursements without the usual hassle;
  • This is a product with incredible scalability. Think about it: Strategic Education currently has 78 corporate partners (they added two new partners in Q1 FY 2025), with those companies totaling 3.9 million employees. WorkForce Edge has currently brought in 2,300 students at both Strayer and Capella. This is about 0.05% of the employees of the partner companies! Imagine if this continues to expand;
  • The direct connection to large employers makes WorkForce Edge a perfect product for a company that focuses so much on the job market. With this in mind, they can expect more enrollments in other employability-focused courses.

Now that you and Strategic have been properly introduced, let’s tackle the big question: is this EdTech in good financial shape?

Does Strategic Education Pass the Test?

Just by looking at it, we can notice some interesting things:

  1. Strategic Education has been a debt-free company since FY 2024. During the period from FY 2020 to FY 2023 the company maintained some debt due to the acquisition of Laureate's businesses in Australia and New Zealand;
  2. The company is also self-financing. If we look at the last decade, Strategic Education has generated positive FCF every year. This has led to them generating a cash surplus every year until FY 2020. This is the good old-fashioned way of an asset-light business with low Capex needs. After this year, they have been running a cash deficit that has been decreasing as they repay their long-term debt, and should start accumulating cash again in FY 2025. Furthermore, they have $182.6 million in cash and equivalents, which for a debt-free company, is a lot of liquidity;
  3. Recent trends (FY 2024) are pointing us towards sustained post M&A growth.

But we’re looking at more recent trends here, right?

In FY 2024, Strategic saw revenues grow nearly 7.7% year-over-year to $1.23 billion, driven primarily by its EdTech services (remember, this includes partnerships with companies, WorkForce Edge and Sophia Learning), while US Higher Education remained flat along with Australia and New Zealand.

It’s clear that while US Higher Education with Capella and Strayer remains the cash cow, the star is EdTech.

In the same period, they reported EBIT of $157.3 million (up 40% year-over-year) and this was largely impacted by increased revenue inflow from the EdTech segment with segment margins reaching 40%. This boosted EBIT margin by nearly 300 bp year-over-year to 12.9%.

But that's not all, normalizing the diluted EPS for the entire FY 2024 we find $4.22. This is the highest EPS in the last decade.

Looking ahead to Q1 FY 2025, does this look like it will continue? Yes, it does.

They reported revenue of $303.6 million (maintaining mid-single-digit growth year-over-year), yet they continued to see the benefits in margins from increased EdTech inflow. For example, adjusted operating income increased 16% year-over-year, and operating profit margin increased to 13.6%. But perhaps the biggest growth was in adjusted earnings per share, which jumped from $1.11 to $1.29.

As I explained, the key to understanding this is in the revenue streams. Just take a look at how that played out in Q1 FY 2025:

  • U.S. Higher Education (Strayer and Capella) revenues were $221 million in the quarter. This represented an increase of 0.8% year-over-year. This revenue stream had an adjusted operating profit margin of 13.6% and an adjusted operating income of $30 million (up 6.9% year-over-year);
  • The EdTech segment saw revenues of $34.3 million in Q1 FY 2025, a very strong 45.2% year-over-year growth. Operating margins in the segment were 40.3%, down 240 bp year-over-year and totaling an adjusted operating profit of $13.8 million (up 37.3% year-over-year);
  • Australia and New Zealand saw revenues of $48.3 million (up 1.9% year-over-year), with an adjusted operating margin of -4.3% and an operating loss of $2.1 million.

B2B: Strategic Education's Gold Mine

B2B partnerships are benefiting Strategic in every way.

In the United States, for example, the percentage of students affiliated with companies increased by approximately 7% year-over-year, with 31% of all enrolled students coming from corporate partners.

Sophia Learning, a major driver of the EdTech segment and a gateway to corporate partnerships (if the company offers some type of short-term, individualized training), increased its total average subscribers by 37% and its revenue by 36%.

As I mentioned, WorkForce Edge already has 78 corporate partners on board—and thanks to it, over 2,300 students have enrolled at Strayer or Capella. That’s a huge 50% year-over-year jump.

This is the heart of the whole thesis.

WorkForce Edge is super scalable and still just getting started. Right now, only about 0.05% of employees from those partner companies are actually enrolled.

In Q1 FY 2025, we got a taste of this with Best Buy Co., Inc. (BBY) joining a full partnership with Strayer University (all full- and part-time Best Buy employees earn a degree at no cost to the employee) and WorkForce Edge.

Dividends and Buybacks? Yes, They Pay!

With strong FCF, Strategic Education typically returns capital to shareholders in the form of dividends and buybacks.

Here are some key facts about this:

  • Strategic has $197 million remaining in repurchase authorization through the end of FY 2025. Last quarter, they used $32 million to repurchase approximately 390,000 shares of common stock, bringing their outstanding shares to 24 million. Assume they return this $197 million during FY 2025, repurchasing between 2 to 2.5 million shares depending on average repurchase price. This would bring their outstanding shares to 21.5 to 22 million, a return of over 8% by the end of the year;
  • An estimated dividend of $2.40. This is a yield of approximately 2.8% with a payout ratio of just over 47%. It is important to note that the company is not a good dividend payer. That is, they have paid consecutive dividends for only 7 years (less than the industry median of 11) and have not increased their dividend since 2019.

If we include dividends and buybacks, we're looking at roughly a 10% return from now until year-end—not too shabby.

But let’s take a look at the valuation and see if this hidden gem is actually trading at a discount compared to its peers.

Discounted Relative to Peers? Let's Find Out

According to SA Quant, Strategic Education scores a B- on Valuation. That’s a solid starting point. However, to locate a price target, we need to select the right companies to compare multiples.

That said, I will use a series of companies from the for-profit higher education sector. They are:

  • Adtalem Global Education Inc. (ATGE);
  • Grand Canyon Education, Inc. (LOPE);
  • Perdoceo Education Corporation (PRDO);
  • Laureate Education, Inc. (LAUR);
  • American Public Education, Inc. (APEI).

Starting with the P/E, we note that the average of these five companies gives us an average multiple of 16.3x. Analysts expect EPS of $5.51 to $5.72 (consensus at $5.63) for the full FY 2025. This would give us a price target of approximately $91.80 for this stock.

Moving on to another equity multiple, the P/S of the comparable companies is 2.6x. Analysts estimate FY 2025 revenue between $1.26 billion and $1.28 billion (consensus $1.27 billion). With roughly 22 million shares outstanding at the end of FY 2025, I expect an RPS of $57.72. That gives us a price target of $150.

Sounds pretty high, doesn’t it? Excluding outliers like Grand Canyon, we have a 2x multiple, which gives us a price target of $115.

Jumping to EV multiples, let’s take a look at EV/EBIT. First, let’s assume an EBIT margin of 10% to 14%. With revenues estimated at $1.27 billion, EBIT would be projected at $127 to $177 million. Other assumptions we could make are about net debt. I expect them to be sitting on at least $200 million in cash and equivalents at the end of FY 2025, with that being negative net debt. And of course, 22 million shares outstanding at the end of the year.

The average comparable multiple for EV/EBIT is 12.3x. Using the low end of the EBIT estimates, we would have a price target of $80—very close to where the stock is trading today. Now, if we consider the high-end, we would have a price target of around $108.

Considering all metrics, we have the following price target perspective for Strategic Education:

A Headwind to Consider: Australia and New Zealand

Revenues from Australia and New Zealand make up around 20% of the company’s total—but that chunk is under some pressure right now.

But why? Simple. Changes in the regulatory environment. Let me explain.

Anthony Albanese, Australia’s Prime Minister, assumed office with a commitment to implementing stricter controls on international student admissions—especially at Aussie universities, where a large share of students come from nearby China. Now, a coalition is pushing to cap international enrollments at 240,000 per year.

That’s not great news for Strategic Education, especially considering they entered the region just five years ago. A large part of their student base there tends to be international, so this policy shift could hit them where it hurts.

Strategic will focus more on Australian and New Zealand students, but we can safely expect enrollments to decline by low to mid-single digits for the rest of the year.

Bottom Line

Putting it all together, we’re looking at a potential upside of 26.5% just from capital gains if the stock hits my $105 price target. Now tack on another 10% or so from dividends and buybacks, and you’ve got a total return approaching 40% by the end of 2025.

With tailwinds from the EdTech segment (particularly from WorkForce Edge partnerships), we have a strong outlook for FY 2025. And perhaps best of all: a squeaky-clean balance sheet capable of both M&A and returning capital to shareholders.

That said, I’m starting my coverage of Strategic Education with a ‘Strong Buy’ rating.

data.title: NYLI Epoch Capital Growth Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The MSCI World Index fell 1.7% over the first quarter of 2025. Equities sold off in the second half of Q1 as enthusiasm faded post Trump's inauguration, replaced instead by unease arising from tariff uncertainty, creeping doubts in the Artificial Intelligence (AI) secular growth story fueled by the release of DeepSeek, softer economic data, and depressed sentiment. Cyclical pockets expressed this hit, with discretionary industries underperforming many defensives. Caution was further indicated by the various downward revisions of growth forecasts by corporate and central banks alike, coupled with negative earnings revisions. The American stock market was the main laggard this quarter as the megacap tech names pulled back on reduced faith in the payoff of AI capex spending. U.S. markets were further plagued by tariff and policy uncertainty, which caused some rotation to favor ex-U.S. investments as well as revision upward of inflation expectations. Europe outperformed, particularly with the added boosts of German expansionary fiscal impulse, accommodative central banks, and a rally by European defense stocks in anticipation of increased military spending. However, European economic indicators remain sluggish, with muted growth forecasts and continued monetary easing attempting to combat it. Asian equity performance was more of a mixed bag over the quarter, with China and Hong Kong the standouts and Japan, Taiwan, and South Korea trailing behind. China and Hong Kong indices were particularly propelled by technology boons like DeepSeek's announced AI advancements and fiscal support for the sector. The region's economic data, however, reflected a slight claw back in retail sales and industrial output, but continues to show labor market weakness. The top performing sectors this quarter included energy (+10.1%), utilities (+7.2%), and financials (+6.1%).

Information technology (-11.9%), consumer discretionary (-10.3%), and communications services (-4.4%) all lagged this quarter due in part to the correction in the Magnificent 7** names.

**(Alphabet (Google)(GOOG)(GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), Meta platforms (Facebook) (META), Microsoft (MSFT), Nvidia (NVDA) & Tesla (TSLA))

However, a contingent deferred sales charge of 1.00% may be imposed on certain redemptions made within 18 months of the date of purchase on shares that were purchased without an initial sales charge.

Portfolio Highlights

  • The NYLI Epoch Capital Growth Fund (the “Fund”) underperformed its benchmark by ~1.0% in the first quarter. This consisted of a negative impact from factor exposures, that was partially offset by a positive contribution from stock-specific risk. Industry and style factors both detracted, while country and currency exposures contributed positively.
  • Over the course of the quarter, the trades we made, together with the impact of the varying performance across stocks and sectors, caused our weights in the health care and consumer staples sectors to rise by 2.5% and 0.7%, respectively. Meanwhile, our weight in the information technology sector fell by 2.0% and our weight in consumer discretionary fell by 1.5%. As of March 31st, our biggest overweight was in health care (+8.2%), followed by consumer discretionary (6.7%) and industrials (+4.5%). We were underweight in almost every other sector, led by financials (-6.0%) and information technology (-4.3%), followed by utilities (-2.7%), real estate (-2.2%), communication services (-2.1%), and materials (-1.7%). As we always note, none of the sector weightings reflect a top-down view, but instead result from the individual holdings identified by our investment process, together with the overall risk management that takes place during portfolio construction.
  • On the other hand, something that very much is intentional is the Fund's wide Return on Invested Capital - Weighted Average Cost of Capital (ROIC-WACC) spread compared to the benchmark. As of March 31st, the Fund's weighted-median aggregate spread was 18.4%, compared to 7.8% for the MSCI World Index.

What Helped:

  • Looking at country and currency exposures, the underweight to the United States helped performance amid the sell-off, in addition to overweights to Spain and China. The underweight to Germany was the top detracting country exposure as the administration kicked up fiscal support for the economy. The overweights to the Swedish krona, Mexican peso, and British pound contributed to active returns as well.
  • Positively contributing industry exposures included the overweight to Health Care Providers & Services and underweights to Technology Hardware and Software. The positive exposure to profitability contributed 0.2% in active return.
  • As for stock-specific risk, BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF), Chemed (CHE), BayCurrent (OTCPK:BYCRF), and Yum Brands (YUM) all contributed positively to active performance during Q1, coupled with the portfolio's underweight positions in the Magnificent 7. BYD released a few positive announcements in February that boosted its share price, including strong unit sales growth and the advancement of its AI-powered smart driving system. The company also continues to enjoy the windfalls of competitive pricing and favorable valuation, making it a preferred source of Electric Vehicle (EV) exposure over peers.

What Hurt:

  • Industry exposures collectively detracted 1.5%. The underweights to Aerospace & Defense and Insurance, in addition to the overweight to Life Sciences Tools & Services, inflicted the largest negative impact on active return. The underweight to Banks also detracted. Within styles, negative exposures to momentum and value detracted, along with the positive market sensitivity exposure. Other all, The underweights to the Japanese yen and euro detracted from active returns.
  • The stocks that caused the largest detractions were Deckers Outdoor Corporation (DECK), eMemory, Arista Networks (ANET), and West Pharmaceutical (WST). Deckers shares have been under pressure year-to-date, largely reflecting fiscal fourth quarter guidance offered at the end of January that was more cautious than the market had anticipated. We believe the lower-than-expected guidance is largely a function of the timing of new product launches and suggests a lower growth rate than the company has had in recent years. An uncertain consumer environment and the prospect of tariffs may have also weighed on shares in the near-term. We continue to have faith in the company due to the Hoka brand's robust innovation slate to be launched through 2025, meaningful international expansion opportunities, and healthy profitability in the form of high ROIC.

Top Contributors and Detractors (%)

Outlook and Positioning:

  • Equity markets began the year on a positive note, but the trend changed dramatically from February 19th as the focus shifted to trade wars. This weakness was initially concentrated in U.S. markets but has now spread to all major equity markets globally.
  • Overall, we believe the trade war will proceed in three stages, beginning with tariff hikes. The second stage, which we've just entered, is about negotiating deals, with the stock market likely to bottom once real progress is signaled. On the other hand, lack of progress means the "America alone" scenario and suggests further downside. The third stage, likely to begin in Q3, emphasizes Trump's pro-growth agenda, which includes tax cuts, deregulation, shrinking the government, and pursing pro-tech policies. If this, admittedly optimistic, scenario is realized, equities could be significantly above today's level by year-end.
  • Turning to market fundamentals, consensus earnings forecasts do not yet adequately incorporate recent developments in the trade war. For example, the S&P 500 Index is still expected to grow Earnings Per Share (EPS) this year by 12% (marked down from 15% last month) and 14% (unchanged) in 2026. The corresponding EPS growth estimates are slightly lower for Europe (7% and 11%), Japan (10% and 9%) and China (8% and 12%), with these forecasts only revised marginally lower over the last month. It is highly likely that consensus 2025 EPS growth estimates will be slashed over coming months.
  • Regarding valuations, the 2025 12-month Forward Price Earnings ratio for the S&P 500 Index is currently 19x, just slightly above the historical mean of 17x. Similarly, the tech sector is now trading at 23x, down from 29x two months ago. Markets outside the U.S. look quite attractive, with Europe at 14x, Japan 13x, and China 12x. All are still trading at exceptionally large discounts to the U.S.
  • What does all this mean for the equity market outlook? Trade war induced uncertainty is likely to be with us for a few more months, if not quarters. However, Trump 2.0 tax cuts, a pro-tech bias and deregulation measures, as well as the "Fed put," amount to powerful reasons to expect the S&P 500 Index to appreciate by 10% before year-end.

The opinions expressed are those of Epoch Investment Partners, Inc. as of the date of this report and are subject to change. There is no guarantee that any forecast made will come to pass. This material does not constitute investment advice and is not intended as an endorsement of any specific investment.

data.title: Reserve Bank Of Australia Preview: Next Week Provides Another Window To Cut Rates
data.description:
By Deepali Bhargava & Francesco Pesole

Likely to be another high conviction rate cut

We expect the Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) to cut the cash rate by 25bp to 3.6% at the upcoming policy meeting next week. Recent data flows on growth and inflation have been softer than expected.

The previous rate adjustment by the RBA was not only a reduction but a decisive one, with discussions centred around whether the cut should be 25bp or 50bp. In alignment with other central banks in the region, the RBA adopted a dovish stance on the growth trajectory due to tariff uncertainties and also revised its inflation forecasts downward.

Weaker growth and softer inflation reinforce our view

What adds conviction to our call next week is a much softer-than-expected CPI inflation reading for May. Headline CPI decreased from 2.4% in April 2025 to 2.1% in May 2025, nearing the lower end of the central bank's target range of 2-3%. High trimmed mean inflation reflecting high services inflation, which has been a more persistent issue, also declined to 2.4% year-over-year, indicating substantial progress in controlling inflation from a peak of 4.4% year-over-year one year ago.

First-quarter GDP growth was weak too, with quarter-on-quarter growth at just 0.2%, falling below expectations. Softer private consumption growth was partly seasonal and due to extreme weather events, but public spending has had the largest negative impact on growth since the September quarter of 2017, and net trade also dragged GDP growth lower.

However, employment data was strong, with wage growth rising to a solid 3.4% year-over-year in the first quarter. In May 2025, despite a small drop in overall job growth, full-time employment rose by about 39,000, which partly offset losses in part-time employment. Reduced underemployment and strong demand for hours worked also point to a healthy labour market.

Adding one more rate cut to the 2025 profile

Overall, the global backdrop on tariffs remains uncertain, and both headline and trimmed inflation have been firmly on a downtrend, remaining close to the midpoint of the 2-3% range over the next few quarters.

The labour data could start to turn as well. In the previous monetary policy statement, members observed that the rate of voluntary job turnover had declined and that, anecdotally, the focus of wage bargaining and employment disputes had tilted in favour of workers seeking greater job security. This might see wage growth slow more noticeably than currently forecast.

Given that the downside risks to both growth and inflation are more dominant at this stage, we are adding a 25bp rate cut to our 2025 profile, taking rates to a terminal level of 3.1% by the end of 2025.

AUD/USD may be close to a peak

Risks for AUD around the 9 July US tariff deadline are no greater than for other high-beta currencies, and certainly not more significant than for USD.

AUD has experienced the largest drop in short-term swap rates across G10 since the start of the year: 65bp in the two-year tenor. Although a host of external drivers has sometimes obscured the FX impact, the sensitivity of AUD to rate differentials has increased, as reflected in our G10 short-term valuation model. While this is primarily a function of Fed repricing, a quieter backdrop on tariffs – barring any major escalation in July – means central bank policy is regaining its place as the main driver of relative value in risk-exposed currencies.

AUD front-end swap rates have fallen the most in G10 this year

Markets are currently pricing in 17bp of easing for this month’s RBA meeting and 78bp by year-end. We believe a cut next week could trigger a broader dovish repricing along the AUD OIS curve, with a more visible impact on AUD. On this basis, we expect AUD to lag behind NZD and potentially other high-beta currencies where there is less room for front-end rates to fall, such as SEK.

AUD/USD came close to touching our near-term target of 0.660, with the move still almost entirely driven by US developments. With US inflation, in our view, set to rebound in the coming months and drive a hawkish repricing in USD rates, we expect a recovery in USD. Coupled with a more dovish RBA – though this remains a secondary driver – we expect AUD/USD to move back to 0.650 this summer.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: Cameco: The King Of Nuclear Ready, Thanks To AI And Geopolitics
data.description:
Why Cameco Is the Big Bet on Nuclear

Cameco Corporation (NYSE:CCJ) is one of the best opportunities to invest in the nuclear revival, destined to become a pillar of the global energy transition and security of supply. The geopolitical crisis has highlighted the vulnerability of traditional energy supply chains, pushing governments in the US, Europe, and Asia to reevaluate the role of nuclear as a stable source to ensure energy autonomy. The real AI revolution is not only technological but also energetic. The explosion in electricity demand linked to the growth of artificial intelligence and data centers is creating unprecedented pressure on the need for new low-emission production capacity.

Its strengths are certainly:

  1. First-tier uranium reserves in safe jurisdictions such as Canada.
  2. Long-term contract portfolio that offers visibility on revenues even in scenarios of spot price volatility.
  3. 49% strategic participation in Westinghouse, thus positioning itself as a global leader capable of providing not only raw materials, but also technology and services for new nuclear projects. With solid growth guidance for the coming years, robust cash flows, and a balance sheet capable of financing expansion without significant dilution, Cameco offers investors privileged exposure to a multi-year nuclear investment cycle.

Overview

Cameco is a leading global player in the uranium market, with integrated operations across the entire nuclear fuel cycle. The company operates tier-one mines such as McArthur River/Key Lake and Cigar Lake in Canada, which are considered among the richest and lowest-cost in the world, and engages in refining, conversion, and fuel fabrication through its Fuel Services division.

Completing its vertical integration, Cameco holds a 49% interest in Westinghouse Electric Company, a global leader in nuclear reactor technology and services, as detailed in the company’s Q1 2025 presentation.

As of December 31, 2024, the company controls more than 485 million pounds of proven and probable reserves, providing a sustainable production base for decades. The long-term contract portfolio is expected to average approximately 28 million pounds per year through 2029, with particularly strong levels in the 2025-2027 period.

Cameco earns the majority of its revenues in US dollars, providing a partial natural hedge against Canadian dollar volatility. Its disciplined strategy of signing contracts only under favorable market conditions maximizes margins and reduces dependence on spot price fluctuations.

Finally, its participation in Global Laser Enrichment provides an additional competitive advantage, positioning Cameco in key segments of the fuel cycle with innovative technologies.

Growing Earnings and Long-Term Contracts: How Cameco is Beating Expectations

Cameco’s Q1 2025 results confirmed the company’s operational and financial strength, demonstrating the resilience of the business even in the context of volatile uranium spot prices. According to the official results release, the company recorded:

  • Revenues of CAD 789 million, up 24% compared to Q1 2024.
  • Adjusted Net Earnings of CAD 70 million, +52% y/y.
  • Adjusted EBITDA of CAD 353 million, +5% y/y.

Uranium

The Uranium segment generated revenues of CAD 619 million (+10% y/y) thanks to an average realized price of CAD 89.12/lb (+15% y/y). Adjusted EBITDA was CAD 286 million, slightly down (-6%) mainly due to the lower contribution of the Inkai JV due to the temporary stop in January. However, long-term contracts allowed Cameco to benefit from prices above the spot market, confirming the effectiveness of the disciplined contracting strategy, as we said before.

Fuel Services

The Fuel Services segment showed exceptional expansion with revenues up to CAD 135 million (+88% y/y) and adjusted EBITDA to CAD 75 million (+200% y/y), thanks to sales volumes up 60% and a 17% increase in the average realized price (see presentation on the website) This improvement reflects the recovery of downstream activities and the consolidation of demand from utilities.

Segment Westinghouse

The contribution of the 49% stake in Westinghouse recorded a net loss reduced to -62 million CAD compared to -123 million a year earlier, with adjusted EBITDA increasing to 92 million CAD (+19% y/y). Cameco expects Westinghouse adjusted EBITDA (Cameco share) in the range of 355-405 million USD for 2025, confirming the progressive integration and the ability to generate significant cash flows from this strategic participation.

Guidance & Outlook

The production guidance for 2025 remains unchanged with 36 million pounds total expected from the McArthur River/Key Lake and Cigar Lake sites, while the Inkai JV revised the 2025 target to 8.3 million pounds (100% basis), of which 3.7 million from Cameco share, after the temporary suspension in January.

Valuation & Return Potential

According to Bloomberg Terminal, Cameco is currently trading at an estimated forward EV/EBITDA of 20.91x for FY2026.

I have developed two scenarios to estimate a fair value consistent with the company's growth prospects.

Baseline (conservative)

  • Estimated EBITDA FY2026: CAD2.071 billion (Bloomberg).
  • Target multiple: 20.91x EV/EBITDA (in line with current market valuations).
  • Enterprise Value (EV) = CAD2,071 million × 20.91 = CAD43,301 million.
  • Estimated net debt FY2026: -CAD 497 million.
  • Equity Value = CAD43,301 million + CAD0.497 million = CAD 43,798 million.
  • The fair value per share = CAD43,798 million / 436 million shares = CAD100.5 share, or $74.4 per share (exchange rate 1 USD = 1.35 CAD).

Potential upside vs. current price (around 71 USD): +5%.

Bull scenario (optimistic)

  • Estimated EBITDA FY2026: we can assume a +15% vs. baseline guidance, in the event of a rise in spot uranium prices above $90/lb and an acceleration of long-term contracts. Both elements are supported by the market trends highlighted by Cameco on its official website and in the Q1 2025 presentation, which show a structural supply deficit expected as early as 2026-2027.
  • These factors make an FY2026 EBITDA of CAD2.381 billion plausible, which reflects an improvement compared to the base guidance thanks to volumes contracted at higher prices and greater use of conversion and fuel services assets.
  • Target multiple: 23x EV/EBITDA, consistent with the historical positive cycles of the sector.
  • EV = CAD2,381 million × 23 = CAD54,763 million
  • Equity Value = CAD54,763 million + CAD 0.497 million = CAD55,260 million.
  • The fair value per share = CAD55,260 million / 436 million shares = CAD126.7 per share, or $93.9 per share.

Potential upside vs. current price: +32%.

AI, Data Centers and the Energy Revolution: The Tailwinds for Uranium

The current macroeconomic and geopolitical context offers Cameco extremely favorable terrain to support uranium demand and strengthen margins.

Energy security and the new geopolitics of energy

The Russia-Ukraine crisis has highlighted the vulnerability of gas and coal supply chains, accelerating the search for solutions independent of geopolitically unstable suppliers.

Structural demand from AI and data centers

According to Goldman Sachs projections, the growth of artificial intelligence will lead to a 160% increase in data center electricity consumption by 2030. This pressure on global electricity demand will make stable and carbon-free energy sources such as nuclear essential, reinforcing the need for long-term contracts for uranium

Global political push for nuclear

The US, the European Union, China, India, and several Middle Eastern countries have announced new programs to build large reactors or Small Modular Reactors (SMR) within the next decade. The EU has included nuclear in the green taxonomy, confirming the political commitment to consider it an integral part of the energy transition.

Rigid supply and long-term contracts

Cameco highlights in the Q1 2025 presentation that the market is set to enter a structural deficit between uranium supply and demand as early as 2026, due to the lack of new significant mining projects and the operational difficulties of producers in geopolitically unstable areas such as Kazakhstan.

Risks & Opportunities

Key Risks

  • High Valuations: The company is priced at a forward EV/EBITDA of 20.91x, above its historical average of 18-19x (Bloomberg Terminal). Any disappointment on uranium prices or delays in signing new contracts could lead to a contraction in multiples.
  • Geopolitics Inkai JV: Cameco holds a 40% stake in the Inkai joint venture in Kazakhstan, a country with geopolitical risks, as demonstrated by the temporary suspension of operations in January 2025.
  • Regulatory or Construction Delays: New reactor or SMR projects may be slowed down for regulatory, financial, or public sentiment reasons, postponing expected near-term uranium demand growth.
  • Spot Price Volatility: While long-term contracts offer protection, a prolonged decline in spot prices could impact market sentiment and cause utilities to delay new deals, as explained in the “Market Dynamics” section of the Cameco website.

Key Opportunities

  • Rising Uranium Prices: Spot prices could rise above $90/lb in the next 12–24 months, generating margin leverage.
  • New Long-Term Contracts: Cameco stated in its Q1 2025 presentation that it has a significant pipeline of new LT contract deals (Investor Presentation), which could be accretive to EBITDA and improve future revenue visibility.
  • Westinghouse as a Catalyst: Westinghouse’s 49% stake provides direct exposure to new demand for reactor technology, both large and SMR, with growth opportunities in emerging markets and in existing reactor upgrade programs.

Strong Buy Rating: Cameco As a Strategic Investment for the Future of Energy

Cameco is the company par excellence if you want exposure to the uranium sector. The company benefits from a long-term contract portfolio that protect revenues even in volatile phases and a solid financial position, as evidenced by the results of the first quarter of 2025 and the confirmed guidance. The stake in Westinghouse guarantees additional exposure to the positive cycle of construction and renewal of nuclear reactors, while the market dynamics signal a structural deficit in uranium supply already from 2026, as illustrated in the Investor Presentation and in the Market Overview section of the company website.

Although current valuations already discount some potential, according to my calculations in the bull scenario, a further increase in uranium spot prices and an acceleration of LT contracts is possible that would push the fair value per share up to $94, with a potential upside of more than 30% from current levels. In the context of growing global electricity demand, driven by AI and digitalization, and political support for nuclear in several areas of the world, Cameco is well positioned to benefit from favorable secular trends.

For these reasons, I think Cameco deserves a Strong Buy rating, with a positive medium-long term view, based on solid fundamentals, macro catalysts, and the ability to generate value through disciplined business management. Recommended maximum weight of 5% in a well-diversified portfolio.

data.title: Capitalize On Rising Demand: Why Micron Is Poised For Growth
data.description:
I gave Micron (NASDAQ: MU) a buy recommendation in my last article on this stock due to the company's potential opportunity in High Bandwidth Memory ("HBM"), which could be massive, the likelihood that the memory industry is in an upcycle, and the favorable forward PEG ratio indicating the stock has substantial potential upside. The company's third-quarter earnings report confirmed those theses.

As a result, the stock has increased by 82.41% since my recommendation on April 21, 2025, compared to the S&P 500's (SPX) 20.71% rise as of the July 2 market close. Looking forward, the ongoing recovery in the DRAM (Dynamic Random Access Memory) chip market is the biggest reason that investors should remain interested in this stock. Generally, as the DRAM market performs, Micron's stock tends to follow suit.

The company's third quarter of FY 2025 demonstrated robust DRAM bit demand driven by AI in the data center, and Micron is shipping the chips to meet that demand. Its DRAM bit shipments grew 20% sequentially during the third quarter. A bit is a unit of digital storage capacity. Bit growth refers to the total number of memory bits that Micron manufactures and/or sells.

HBM, a specialized portion of the DRAM market, is growing even faster than the overall DRAM market. Based on second quarter figures, HBM has a run rate exceeding $6 billion. Chief Business Officer ("CBO") Sumit Sadana said on the company's third quarter fiscal year ("FY") 2025 earnings call, "So in terms of [FY] 2026 HBM, we do expect that HBM bit growth in '26 over 2025 calendar year will be significantly faster than the overall DRAM bit growth."

This article will discuss the market demand for DRAM and the company's inventory dynamics. It will also review the company's third-quarter FY2025 report, risks, valuation, and why I still maintain a buy recommendation for Micron.

Early stages of a DRAM memory market upcycle

Micron raised its DRAM bit demand outlook for calendar year 2025 to the high teens from mid-teens. Increasing bit demand growth indicates that the market is consuming more memory, a sign of growing demand. When Micron ships more DRAM bits to meet increased demand, the increased volume can drive its total revenue higher, even if the price per bit declines slightly. Additionally, suppose the bit demand skyrockets and outstrips manufacturers' ability to supply the bits. In that case, manufacturers gain leverage to maintain or even increase Average Selling Prices (ASPs), leading to higher gross margins. Investors view increased bit demand as a positive sign for the memory industry and Micron. As demand for memory increases, the market often awards companies in the memory industry a higher valuation.

CBO Sadana said the following on the third quarter earnings call regarding demand:

The strength in our view of the AI [Artificial Intelligence] business, i.e., the data center growth, continues to be pretty robust. And our view of the AI demand is certainly supportive of the forecast that we have provided you for bit growth in calendar '25. And the other positive thing that has happened over the last 3 months is that we are seeing, after many, many quarters of a very challenging environment in the broad distribution markets, industrial markets and some parts of the automotive market, we are now starting to see really good improvements in demand in these broad distribution and industrial markets. And that is also providing a tailwind to the overall calendar '25 bit growth estimates.

The above commentary singles out the reasons behind the increased bit demand, which boil down to continued high demand from AI data centers and a rebound in areas that have been weak recently, namely, memory and storage solutions for industrial applications, memory and storage products sold for consumer electronics, which can include items like personal computers, mobile devices (smartphones, tablets), gaming systems, and other consumer-oriented devices, and memory solutions for the automotive industry.

Another positive factor on the horizon is that the company has gained leverage to increase pricing moving forward. When an analyst asked a question during the third-quarter earnings call about November's gross margins, Chief Financial Officer Mark Murphy said:

We've talked about our inventories are very lean. We're a bit constrained as we look out to that [November] quarter. So we're focused on the best pricing decisions.

"Inventories are very lean" means Micron doesn't have a lot of excess DRAM supply, putting the company in a favorable position for price negotiations with customers in a high-demand market. "We're focused on the best pricing decisions" means the company will choose to sell to customers from whom it can gain the highest price, thereby maximizing gross margins. Mark Murphy's commentary suggests that investors should expect Micron's gross margins to rise in the November quarter. If gross margins do indeed increase solidly in future quarters, the market will likely award the stock with even higher valuations.

Company fundamentals

Micron increased total revenue by 15% sequentially and 37% year-over-year to $9.3 billion, beating analysts' estimates by $448.25 million.

One of the reasons I only discussed DRAM memory recovery in the previous section and didn't discuss NAND (flash memory) is that Micron generates most of its revenue from DRAM. As the above image shows, 76% of the company's revenue comes from DRAM. I will discuss NAND recovery more in the risk section.

The sequential increase in Bit shipments by 20% in both markets indicates robust demand and may lead to a corresponding revenue increase. However, ASPs decreasing sequentially in both markets is a bad indicator for gross margins, and if ASPs decline enough, it could offset the benefits to revenue from increased bit shipments.

Micron reports GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) and non-GAAP metrics. However, it emphasizes non-GAAP metrics in its investor presentation to highlight its core profitability. Non-GAAP metrics exclude stock-based compensation ("SBC"), gains and losses from settlements, gains and losses from debt prepayments, restructuring and asset impairments, patent license charges, and the estimated tax effects of these items.

The company has a quarterly and annual SBC as a percentage of revenue of 2.72% and 3.32%, respectively, which is relatively low.

Micron's non-GAAP gross margin increased by 11 points year-over-year to 39%. Its non-GAAP operating expense (OpEx) increased by 12.70% year-over-year to $1.1 billion. Revenue growing faster than OpEx suggests that operating margins should expand rapidly. The company's third quarter FY 2025 non-GAAP operating margins expanded by nine points to 23.65%. Micron's non-GAAP diluted earnings per share ("EPS") increased by 208% year-over-year to $1.91, beating analysts' estimates by $0.31.

The company's trailing 12-month ("TTM") cash from operations ("CFO") to sales is 44.95%, meaning that for every $1 of sales over the last year, the company generated $0.45.

Micron's third quarter FY 2025 TTM CFO was $15.20 billion. Its third quarter FY 2025 TTM capital expenditure ("CapEx") increased 98% year-over-year to $13.32 billion. Generally, when a chip manufacturer increases CapEx, it means it has become confident in future demand and has increased investment to meet that demand. An increase in CapEx usually precedes future revenue growth. The company's third quarter FY 2025 TTM free cash flow ("FCF") increased from -$1.376 billion in the third quarter of 2024 to $1.881 billion. Micron's third-quarter FY 2025 TTM FCF margin is 5.6%, representing a sequential expansion of 3.7 points.

The company's third quarter FY 2025 CFO increased 84% year over year to $4.6 billion. Its third quarter FY 2025 CapEx increased 22% year-over-year to $2.7 billion. Micron's third-quarter FY 2025 adjusted FCF increased 347% to $1.9 billion.

The company ended the third quarter of FY 2025 with $12.2 billion of cash and short-term investments. When including its untapped credit facility, the company has $15.7 billion of liquidity. Chief Financial Officer Murphy said on the third quarter FY 2025 earnings call in his prepared remarks:

During fiscal Q3, we refinanced our $900 million 2027 notes with $1.75 billion in new notes maturing in fiscal years 2033 and 2036. We closed the quarter with $15.5 billion of debt, maintaining low net leverage and a weighted average debt maturity of 2032.

Micron's debt-to-equity ratio is 0.32, indicating that the company conservatively finances its operations with a higher proportion of equity than debt. Its debt-to-EBITDA ratio is 0.93, indicating that the company can repay its debt without stress.

Management's forecast for fourth-quarter FY 2025 revenue is $10.7 billion, with a range of $300 million, exceeding analysts' estimates of $9.89 billion before the earnings announcement. Following the earnings announcement, analysts raised their estimates to $10.73 billion.

If the company reaches its fourth-quarter FY gross margin forecast of 42%, its gross margins will expand by 5.5 points. Suppose the company reaches its diluted EPS of $2.50; its diluted EPS will grow 112% year over year.

This outlook is overall favorable. Since DRAM is a higher-margin business for Micron, revenue weighted toward DRAM is beneficial. Although operating expenses are increasing, investors are likely to tolerate this increase because it is primarily due to increased investment in R&D (research and development) for future technology nodes and HBM. This type of spending is necessary for long-term growth, innovation, and maintaining a competitive edge in high-growth areas, such as HBM. As long as increased OpEx is not heavily weighted towards G&A (general and administrative) increases, which mostly go to overhead, investors will generally tolerate the OpEx increase.

"Tight DRAM inventories" and "significantly reduced NAND inventories" are very favorable indicators. When Micron exhibits low or reduced inventories in a high-demand market, it means that robust demand is surpassing the company's ability to supply the market and strengthening management's ability to negotiate higher prices for sales of its DRAM and NAND products. Higher prices translate to higher revenue and gross margin.

Valuation

Micron's trailing 12-month ("TTM") price-to-sales (P/S) ratio is 4.049, which is above its three-, five-, seven-, and ten-year medians, indicating a potential sign of overvaluation.

Micron's Enterprise Value ("EV") to EBITDA ratio is 8.446, below its three-year median of 10.33 (the bull case), but higher than its five-, seven-, and ten-year medians (the bear case).

In my previous article, the stock traded below all its EV/EBITDA medians. I only calculated where it would trade at its three-year median at the time, as that figure represented the most optimistic scenario. In the calculated bull case scenario from April, I obtained an EV of $151.851 billion and a stock price of $129.73, which was 88.5% above Micron's March 18 closing price of $68.80 and remained above the closing price on July 1 of $120.89.

When I recalculated the bull case of where the EV and stock price would be today if it traded at its three-year EV-to-EBITDA median using the figures the company reported as of the close of its fiscal third quarter on May 29, 2025, I derived an EV of $161.40 billion and a stock price of $144.36, which is a 19.41% rise above its July 1 closing price. The five-, seven-, and ten-year medians are now the bear case. If it were to sell at the extreme bear case, a ten-year EV/EBITDA of 6.034, it would have an EV of $91.76 billion and a stock price of $82.07, representing a 32% decline from its July 1 closing price.

Micron's TTM GAAP price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio is 21.98, below the Information Technology sector median of 29.21, a potential sign of undervaluation.

One reason the stock price may have risen recently is that the market had not priced in an estimated EPS growth rate of over 500% in FY 2025. Even after the recent price move upwards, the company has a 2025 forward P/E to growth ("PEG") ratio of 0.03, calculated by dividing the 2025 forward P/E of 15.45 by an estimated EPS growth rate of 501.91%. Generally, the market considers a PEG ratio below 1.0 as a sign of an undervalued stock.

Micron's FY 2026 forward PEG ratio is 0.185. Suppose Micron sold at a forward FY 2026 PEG of 1.0, its stock price would be $650.45, a 438% rise above the July 1 stock price. Micron has tremendous upside potential. However, the market is unlikely to award the stock a PEG ratio of 1.0 because of the cyclicality of the memory business. When forecasting the future of the memory business, it's best to use a pencil rather than a pen because the weather forecast can quickly change from bright and sunny to heavy rain in an instant.

Micron's price-to-FCF (P/FCF) is 73.63, well above its five-year median of 9.454. In my last article about Micron, I stated:

Usually, when a stock's P/FCF sells above its median, the market considers it overvalued. However, interpreting the P/FCF of companies in a cyclical industry requires context. When a cyclical company is peaking, or investors expect revenue growth and FCF to slow soon, a high P/FCF or a P/FCF above its median could signal overvaluation. However, suppose a cyclical company is in the early stages of a recovery. In that case, the market may undervalue the FCF growth that the company will generate as demand for its products or services increases. Micron may be in the early stages of a recovery in the memory cycle, with rising demand for HBM chips driven by AI adoption serving as a significant catalyst for FCF growth. If FCF grows faster than the stock price, its P/FCF could drop below its median, and the stock could quickly go from looking overvalued to undervalued. In my opinion, the market underestimates the company's potential FCF growth.

At the time of my previous article, the company's P/FCF was 128.52. The P/FCF decline to 73.63 signals that Micron's FCF is growing faster than its stock price. If the company is indeed in the early stages of a memory cycle recovery, its P/FCF may continue down each quarter as the stock grows into its valuation.

Risks

Although the company relies less on the NAND market, it's still an essential component of Micron's business. Although the NAND market exhibits decent growth, its market dynamics are less healthy than those in the DRAM market. Chief Financial Officer Murphy said on the third quarter FY 2025 earnings call:

The NAND volumes were very strong. So we're benefiting from some more loading there. But still, we're very careful about capacity in that market because that part of the business, the market environment is challenging. So again, we talk about low CapEx levels, careful about node transition, where our bits are going and premium products in that market and watching inventories carefully. Inventories improved in NAND actually in the quarter, but they remain less healthy than DRAM, which DRAM, we actually expect to be below target by the end of the year.

Micron must carefully manage the amount of NAND it supplies to the market, as oversupplying the market risks lowering the prices it can charge its customers. In other words, while DRAM is firmly in recovery, the NAND market is in much earlier stages of recovery, which could quickly reverse if Micron and other NAND suppliers don't carefully manage their inventories.

The risk of oversupply and pricing pressure in the NAND segment remains. If pricing in the NAND market collapses, it would be detrimental to Micron. The company's overall gross margins could decline, even if its DRAM business continues to do well. Additionally, collapsing NAND prices may force the company to write down its inventories, as unsold inventory could rapidly become obsolete. Inventory write-downs would adversely impact its operating margins. Investors would likely notice the declining profitability and revalue the stock accordingly.

Although the DRAM market is currently in an upturn, the memory market is highly cyclical, meaning upturns can quickly turn into downturns. For instance, a downturn in the global economy could quickly alter the dynamics of the DRAM memory market to an unfavorable one. Additionally, if DRAM memory manufacturers become undisciplined and expand too aggressively, prices could erode, leading to a decline in profitability. Investors follow industry dynamics, and once they observe the declining health of the DRAM market, they will quickly sell off the stock.

Micron mentions in its guidance that it doesn't include the impact of any potential future tariffs. CBO Sumit Sadana noted in the earnings call, "there is uncertainty related to tariffs and whatever may hence happen to the macro environment." Although the U.S. has made some progress in bilateral trade negotiations with certain countries, there remains potential for tariffs on others that could harm Micron's business by disrupting supply chains, altering customer purchasing patterns, and increasing manufacturing or shipping costs.

Micron Remains a Buy

This stock is not for everyone. Risk-averse investors may want to avoid investing in it due to uncertainty about how long the memory bull market will last and how much upside Micron will achieve from a favorable market. However, investors seeking a potentially high-return stock over the short term may consider this investment. To give investors an idea of the potential upside in a DRAM bull market, let's examine the period when the DRAM market experienced an upturn at the beginning of 2020, which continued through the end of 2021 or the beginning of 2022. Micron's closing price on March 16, 2020, was $33.70. The stock reached an intraday price of $96.75 on December 29, 2021, representing a 187% increase in less than two years. So, Micron's stock price can rise substantially in a DRAM bull market.

Some believe that the current DRAM bull market can last two years or more, driven by the extraordinarily high demand from AI data centers. Analysts' revenue and EPS estimates imply that the current memory market will last until FY 2027. Barring a dramatic decline in the global economy, that assessment of the bull market's length may be correct. If the DRAM bull market lasts for an additional two years, I believe investors who purchase now could achieve more than 100% upside over the next two years. Micron is a buy for risk-tolerant investors looking for a high-return investment over the next one to two years.

data.title: A Key Pattern Is Forming In Silver (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Jesse Colombo

Although silver has been quiet in recent weeks, leaving investors puzzled, a bullish chart pattern now appears to be taking shape - indicating that the rally may soon resume.

For context, both gold and silver have been in healthy sideways consolidations, taking a breather after strong runs earlier this year. This kind of pause is typical in financial markets, especially during the quieter summer months when trading activity slows as traders head off on vacation. Let’s take a closer look at the charts.

About a month ago, silver finally broke out above two key resistance zones that had capped its gains for the past year: first the $32–$33 zone, and then the $34–$35 zone. This breakout is a strong indication that silver’s bull market is gaining momentum.

While silver has spent the past few weeks in a healthy consolidation, it has managed to hold onto its gains — a very constructive sign. I’m now watching for a breakout from this consolidation, which I believe will set the stage for a rapid move to $40 an ounce and beyond.

Interestingly, if you take a closer look, the recent consolidation actually resembles a classic bull flag pattern — exactly what you want to see in a strong uptrend. This pattern indicates further gains are likely on the horizon, though a decisive breakout to the upside with strong volume is needed for confirmation.

It’s also worth noting that silver often rallies in this step-like fashion, moving from one technical pattern to the next.

For example, last month’s $4.50 per ounce surge was preceded by a well-defined triangle pattern that formed in April and May. The current bull flag appears to be continuing this pattern of behavior, making it an exciting setup to watch for us silver bulls.

In addition to tracking COMEX silver futures, I also closely monitor silver priced in euros. This helps strip out the influence of U.S. dollar fluctuations and often provides a clearer view of the underlying trend. Euro-denominated silver also tends to respect key €1 increments—such as €30, €31, and €32—which often act as support and resistance levels.

Similar to COMEX silver futures, silver priced in euros has struggled with two major resistance zones: €29–€30 and €31–€32. Recently, it managed to break through the €29–€30 level, but its first attempt to clear €31–€32 was short-lived, as silver retreated in tandem with a strengthening euro.

The encouraging news is that silver is once again pushing into the €31–€32 zone and looks poised for another breakout attempt — especially if U.S. dollar-priced silver futures (as shown in the earlier charts) break out of its bull flag pattern. Ideally, I’d like to see a confirmed breakout in euros as well, which would signal that silver’s bull market is broad-based and not merely a result of recent dollar weakness.

I’ve also developed a proprietary indicator called the Synthetic Silver Price Index (SSPI), designed to help validate silver’s price action and filter out potential false breakouts.

The SSPI is calculated as the average of gold and copper prices, with copper scaled by a factor of 540 to prevent gold from dominating the index. Interestingly, even though silver isn’t part of the calculation, the SSPI closely tracks its movements.

Excitingly, the SSPI finally broke out of its 2,800–3,000 trading range over the past few days, where it had been stuck since March — a promising sign for silver.

This breakout suggests a new tailwind for silver, as strong performances in both gold and copper tend to put pressure on arbitrage trading algorithms to buy silver in sympathy.

The move in the SSPI was one of the key confirmations I had been watching for, along with a breakout in silver priced in euros, which I’m still waiting to see.

The consolidation — or bull flag — pattern forming in silver is also clearly visible in silver mining stocks, as reflected in popular exchange-traded funds (ETFs) like SIL, which tracks large silver miners.

A decisive breakout from silver’s bull flag, combined with a high-volume breakout in SIL, is likely to ignite a powerful rally in the silver mining sector. I personally invest in both silver bullion and select mining stocks.

A bull flag pattern is also clearly visible in SILJ, the popular ETF that tracks junior silver mining stocks:

Another key asset I closely monitor to better understand the broader commodity landscape is the U.S. dollar—specifically the U.S. Dollar Index, which has historically moved inversely to commodities, including precious metals. As I’ve recently noted, the Dollar Index broke below the critical 100 support level, which has now flipped into resistance.

This marks a significant technical breakdown that tilts the outlook for the dollar decidedly bearish—while reinforcing a strong bullish backdrop for commodities like gold, silver, platinum, and copper.

Notably, the dollar’s weakness amid recent Middle East turmoil—an environment that typically strengthens safe-haven assets like the dollar—signals deeper underlying fragility.

To summarize, I’m encouraged by the bull flag pattern forming in silver right now, as this classic continuation pattern indicates even further upside ahead — potentially mirroring last month’s sharp $4.50-per-ounce surge in just a few days.

Of course, I always wait for confirmation through a decisive breakout on strong trading volume, which is what I’m watching for now. I’m feeling optimistic and, as always, I’ll keep you all updated.

data.title: Eagle Materials: Headwinds Remain
data.description:
Today, I am putting Eagle Materials Inc. (EXP) in the spotlight for the first time in 2025. When I last looked at this building supplies concern in November of last year, I concluded that article by stating the "shares seem quite vulnerable to some profit taking in the months and quarters ahead."

The stock has dropped some 35% since that research was posted. Are the shares finally in the "buy zone" after that decline? An updated analysis follows below.

Eagle Materials is headquartered in the Lone Star state and has four primary business units: Cement, Concrete and Aggregates, Gypsum Wallboard, and Recycled Paperboard. The company manufactures the mundane items that supply the construction industry via 70 facilities in over 20 states. These products include paperboard, cement and various aggregates. The stock currently trades at around $214.00 a share and sports an approximate market capitalization of $7 billion.

The company has a non-standard fiscal year that commences on April 1st, meaning Eagle is currently in its fiscal 2026 year. The company over the years has grown organically, as well as by joint ventures and small strategic acquisitions. Here is the key activity in the latter two areas in FY2025.

Recent Results:

Eagle Materials posted its Q4 numbers on May 20th. The company delivered non-GAAP earnings of $2.06 a share, some 35 cents a share under the consensus. This was also down seven percent from the fourth quarter of 2024. Adjusted EBITDA fell 9% to $141.2 million. For FY2025, Non-GAAP earnings per share did rise slightly to $13.77 a share, from $13.61 in FY2024. This small increase in EPS was entirely due to stock buybacks as well, which I will cover in a later segment of the article.

Revenues fell by 1.4% on a year-over-year basis to $470.2 million, more than $8 million under expectations. Breaking down performance by key business units.

Fourth quarter Cement revenue, which includes joint venture and intersegment revenue, fell 6% to $214 million. Lower sales volume was partially offset by higher cement sales prices. Notably, operating earnings in this segment dropped 26% to $27.6 million.

Overall revenues from Gypsum Wallboard and Recycled Paperboard declined one percent to $235.2 million. Operating earnings fell two percent to $90.7 million.

Concrete and Aggregates revenue rose 12% from the same period a year ago to $54.3 million. Sales growth was the result of higher aggregates sales volume as well as the contribution of $6.7 million from the two acquired aggregates businesses. However, this segment had an operating loss of $9.4 million in the quarter largely driven by lower concrete volumes.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet:

The analyst community is quite negative on Eagle Materials at the moment. Almost every analyst rating, both Holds and Buys, following first quarter results has contained a downward price target revision. Truist Financial, Stephens ($255 price target, down from $275 previously) and Goldman Sachs ($242 price target, down from $252 previously) reissued Buy ratings. Five analyst firms including Jefferies and Stifel Nicolaus maintained Hold ratings. Their price targets range from $220 to $245 a share.

Approximately four percent of the overall float is currently held short. One insider added nearly $80,000 to their stake in EXP in February and another bought just over $210,000 worth of shares in May. That has been the only insider activity in the shares so far in 2025.

Eagle Materials ended FY2025 with approximately $20 million worth of cash and marketable securities on its balance sheet against $1.22 billion in long-term debt according to the 10-Q filed for the quarter. Leverage has risen slightly since the same period a year ago. The company repurchased $67 million worth of its own shares in the most recently completed quarter and $298 million during FY2025.

Conclusion:

Eagle Materials made $13.94 a share on $2.26 billion in sales in FY2025. The current analyst firm consensus has profits posting a slight rise to $14.06 a share as sales rise to $2.3 billion in FY2026. They see earnings moving up nicely to $15.63 a share on mid-single digit sales growth in FY2027.

This leaves EXP trading at just over 15 times forward earnings and just over three times revenues. The stock pays a small dividend with a just under .5% annual yield. While the price-to-sales ratio is down from over 4.3 times at its recent peak, it is far above where the stock bottomed in 2000 and 2002.

The building materials sector is facing increasing headwinds on multiple fronts. Infrastructure largess provided by the previous administration is ebbing, both the residential and commercial real estate sectors continue to deteriorate, and interest rates remain much higher than they have been in previous years, curtailing new projects.

U.S. Construction spending fell by .3% month-over-month in May after falling .2% in April. On a year-over-year basis, construction spending dropped 3.5%. Key drivers of this decline were private construction projects, investment in residential construction and outlays on new single-family housing projects.

Nor are things likely to get better at least in the short term. A recent survey of homebuilders showed sentiment among single-family homebuilders plummeted to a 2-1/2-year low in June. This survey also forecasts a decline in single-family starts this year.

Given these headwinds and roughly flat revenue and earnings growth projected for this fiscal year, an investment thesis around EXP is not compelling even after the stock has fallen by a third since the last time I looked at this company back in late 2024.

data.description:
This article updates my review of FLRT published in October 2023 in light of current holdings and recent performance.

FLRT strategy

Pacer Pacific Asset Floating Rate High Income ETF (NYSEARCA:FLRT) is an actively managed bond fund launched on 2/18/2015 with the objective to “provide a high level of current income by investing primarily in floating-rate loans of non-investment-grade companies”. It has a 30-day SEC yield of 7.02% and an expense ratio of 0.60%. Distributions are paid monthly.

FLRT was previously known as Pacific Global Senior Loan ETF, and before that as AdvisorShares Pacific Asset Enhanced Floating Rate ETF. The name has changed twice, but objectives, strategies, and policies have remained similar, so I will consider the whole historical price series to be relevant to assess the current strategy. As described in the prospectus by Pacer ETFs, the fund holds.

Senior secured floating rate loans and other adjustable rate securities. Other adjustable rate securities will typically include collateralized loan obligations (CLOs), asset-backed securities (ABS), and commercial mortgage backed securities (CMBS) (…) The Fund may invest up to 20% of its assets in certain other types of debt instruments or securities, including corporate bonds (including floating rate investment grade bonds) and secured or unsecured second lien floating rate loans.

This strategy description is extremely flexible. On the downside, it lacks transparency, like most active funds. In the most recent fiscal year, the portfolio turnover rate was 43%.

Portfolio

The fund has 204 holdings from 183 issuers. US securities represent 68.8% of asset value, and the weighted average maturity is 6.82 years. The heaviest asset category in the portfolio is bank loans with 53.3%, followed by collateralized loan obligations (CLOs) with 31.6%. Other categories are below 7%.

As for credit risk, 22.1% of assets have an investment grade rating (BBB or better). The portfolio is focused on assets rated BB or B (73.6%).
